questions.question_text: A Maryland listing agent receives a low offer while the seller is waiting on a stronger offer expected tomorrow. What must the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Present the low offer promptly to the seller
distractors: Hold the offer until the better one arrives
Reject the offer on the seller's behalf
Inform the buyer the seller is not interested
questions.explanation: Maryland law requires agents to promptly present ALL offers to the seller unless the seller has provided written instructions to the contrary. An agent cannot withhold or delay presenting any offer, regardless of the amount or timing.

questions.question_text: A seller provides written instructions directing the agent not to present offers below $400,000. An offer of $380,000 comes in. What should the agent do?
distractors: Present the offer despite the seller's instructions
Counter at $400,000 without telling the seller
Inform MREC about the seller's restriction
questions.explanation: Maryland permits sellers to give written contrary instructions about which offers to present. When such written instructions exist, the agent may follow them. Without written instructions, all offers must be presented promptly.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent asks the listing agent whether there are competing offers on a property. How should the listing agent respond?
questions.correct_answer: Confirm other offers exist but not disclose terms
distractors: Share the highest offer amount to spur competition
Deny any other offers exist to avoid issues
Refuse to answer any questions about offers
questions.explanation: In Maryland, the listing agent must inform buyers that other offers exist but cannot disclose the specific terms of those offers, unless the seller instructs otherwise. This balances transparency with protecting seller confidentiality.

questions.question_text: When may a Maryland listing agent withhold an offer from the seller?
questions.correct_answer: When the seller gives written contrary instructions
distractors: When the offer is clearly below market value
When the property is already under contract
When the buyer lacks a pre-approval letter
questions.explanation: The only circumstance in which a Maryland listing agent may withhold an offer is when the seller has provided written instructions directing the agent not to present certain offers. Without such written direction, all offers must be presented promptly.

questions.question_text: A Maryland listing agent receives three offers simultaneously. The agent presents only the highest offer to the seller, believing it serves the seller's best interest. Is this proper?
questions.correct_answer: No, all three offers must be presented promptly
distractors: Yes, agents should filter for the best offers
Yes, if the seller previously said to bring strong offers
Only if the other offers are within 10% of asking
questions.explanation: Maryland requires prompt presentation of ALL offers unless the seller has given specific written contrary instructions. A verbal preference for 'strong offers' does not constitute written instructions to withhold offers. The agent must present all three offers to the seller.

questions.question_text: In Maryland, who may receive a commission directly from a client?
distractors: Any licensed salesperson involved in the deal
The associate broker who negotiated the sale
Either the listing agent or buyer's agent
questions.explanation: In Maryland, only a licensed broker may receive a commission directly from a client. Salespersons and associate brokers must receive their compensation through their supervising broker, not directly from the client.

questions.question_text: A Maryland agent agrees to represent both the buyer and seller and will receive compensation from each. What disclosure is required?
questions.correct_answer: Full written disclosure and consent from both parties
distractors: Verbal disclosure to the buyer is sufficient
No disclosure if the amounts are equal
Disclosure only to the supervising broker
questions.explanation: In Maryland, an agent cannot receive compensation from multiple parties in a transaction without full written disclosure to and consent from all parties involved. This ensures transparency and prevents conflicts of interest.

questions.question_text: Are commission rebates to buyers permitted in Maryland?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the rebate is disclosed to all parties
distractors: No, rebates are prohibited by Maryland law
Only for first-time homebuyers
Yes, but only through the title company
questions.explanation: Maryland permits commission rebates to buyers as long as they are properly disclosed to all parties in the transaction. The key requirement is full transparency about the rebate arrangement.

questions.question_text: A Maryland listing broker proposes a net listing where the seller receives $300,000 and the broker keeps any excess. What is the status of this arrangement?
questions.correct_answer: Net listings are disfavored and considered unethical
distractors: Net listings are standard practice in Maryland
Legal only for properties over $500,000
Permitted if approved by MREC in advance
questions.explanation: Net listings are disfavored and considered unethical in Maryland because they create an inherent conflict of interest between the broker's compensation and the seller's best interests. The broker's commission should be freely negotiable but not structured as a net listing.

questions.question_text: A Maryland seller demands that the listing agent reduce their commission rate. Can the agent refuse?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, commission rates are freely negotiable
distractors: No, the seller sets the commission rate
No, MREC sets maximum commission rates
Yes, but only after the listing has expired
questions.explanation: Commission rates in Maryland are freely negotiable between the broker and client. There is no standard or mandated commission rate. Either party may propose or decline a particular rate. The agent is not obligated to reduce their commission simply because the seller requests it.

questions.question_text: A Maryland salesperson places an ad for a listed property using only their personal name and phone number. What rule has been violated?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name must appear in all advertising
distractors: Agents cannot advertise individual listings
Only the broker may advertise listed properties
Phone numbers are prohibited in print ads
questions.explanation: Maryland prohibits blind ads. All real estate advertising must include the licensed brokerage name. While a salesperson's name may appear, the broker's name must be prominent. Omitting the brokerage name violates advertising requirements.

questions.question_text: What is a blind ad in Maryland real estate?
questions.correct_answer: An ad that omits the licensed brokerage name
distractors: An ad that does not include the property address
An ad placed without the seller's written consent
An ad that fails to include the listing price
questions.explanation: A blind ad is one that does not include the name of the licensed brokerage. Maryland strictly prohibits blind ads in all media, including print, internet, and social media. The brokerage name must be included to ensure the public knows a licensed professional is involved.

questions.question_text: A Maryland real estate team posts a listing on social media using only the team name 'Dream Home Group.' Does this comply with advertising rules?
questions.correct_answer: No, the brokerage name must also appear
distractors: Yes, team names satisfy the brokerage requirement
Yes, social media is exempt from ad rules
Only if the post includes the MLS number
questions.explanation: Maryland allows team names in advertising, but the licensed brokerage name must also appear. Social media posts are subject to the same advertising rules as print and internet ads. Using only the team name without the brokerage name is a violation.

questions.question_text: An agent places a For Sale sign on a Maryland property without asking the homeowner. What requirement was violated?
questions.correct_answer: For Sale signs require the owner's written consent
distractors: Signs must be approved by the local HOA first
Only brokers may authorize For Sale signs
Signs are prohibited on residential properties
questions.explanation: In Maryland, placing a For Sale sign on a property requires the owner's written consent. An agent cannot unilaterally decide to place signage without first obtaining the property owner's permission in writing.

questions.question_text: A Maryland agent's personal website features property listings with the agent's name displayed prominently but the brokerage name in small text at the bottom. Does this comply?
questions.correct_answer: No, the brokerage name must be prominently displayed
distractors: Yes, any mention of the brokerage is sufficient
Yes, websites follow different rules than print ads
Only if the agent is an associate broker
questions.explanation: Maryland requires that the brokerage name be prominent in all advertising, including internet and website advertising. While a salesperson's name may appear, it cannot overshadow the brokerage name. Having the brokerage in small text at the bottom does not satisfy the prominence requirement.
